description:
Cargo.toml line 18 enables only `keyring = { version="3", features=["linux-native", "crypto-rust"] }`. With `linux-native` alone (no `linux-native-sync-persistent` / `-async-persistent`), the keyring crate selects its `keyutils` module as default (verified at keyring-3.6.3/src/lib.rs:207-216). The keyring crate's own keyutils.rs documents this module as 'completely in-memory and will not persist across reboots' (keyutils.rs:21-22) and 'a reboot clears all keyrings' (keyutils.rs:94). `KeyutilsCredentialBuilder::persistence()` returns `UntilReboot` (keyutils.rs:316).

The persistent keyring (man 7 persistent-keyring) IS in-memory and survives logouts within an expiration window — it does NOT survive system reboots. Reboot survival requires Secret Service backing (`linux-native-sync-persistent`), which would re-introduce a daemon dependency that contradicts the README's 'no daemon required' selling point.

Trigger: User reads the README, deploys secretx-keyring expecting reboot survival, reboots, all stored secrets are gone. Bad behavior: silent data loss with no warning. Filed against:
- secretx-keyring/README.md:5 ('survive reboots for a configurable window')
- secretx-keyring/src/lib.rs:13-16 (same claim in module docs)
- secretx-keyring/src/lib.rs:38-39 (Security notes section: 'persistent keyring survives reboots but expires...')

The accurate description for keyutils-only is: 'survives logouts for a configurable window (default 3 days), but is cleared on reboot'. The 'no daemon required' + 'survives reboots' combination is fundamentally incompatible — pick one. If reboot survival is required, switch to `linux-native-sync-persistent` and update docs. If no-daemon is required, fix the docs to remove reboot-survival claims.
title: Docs falsely claim secrets survive reboots — keyutils-only feature set is in-memory-only
